elements.sent-2411-001: Foundation models (FMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks with task-specific fine-tuning, yet full parameter fine-tuning is often computationally prohibitive for large models.
elements.sent-2411-002: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) reduce this cost by introducing low-rank matrices for tuning fewer parameters.
elements.sent-2411-003: While LoRA allows for efficient fine-tuning, it requires significant data for adaptation, making Federated Learning (FL) an appealing solution due to its privacy-preserving collaborative framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, combining LoRA with FL introduces two key challenges: the Server-Side LoRA Aggregation Bias, where server-side averaging of LoRA matrices diverges from the ideal global update, and the Client-Side LoRA Initialization Drift, emphasizing the need for consistent initialization across rounds.
elements.sent-2411-005: Existing approaches address these challenges individually, limiting their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-006: We propose LoRA-FAIR, a novel method that tackles both issues by introducing a correction term on the server while keeping the original LoRA modules, enhancing aggregation efficiency and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-007: LoRA-FAIR maintains computational and communication efficiency, yielding superior performance over state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results on ViT and MLP-Mixer models across large-scale datasets demonstrate that LoRA-FAIR consistently achieves performance improvements in FL settings.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig/illustration.pdf} \caption{Illustration of LoRA-FAIR. Instead of directly averaging the local LoRA modules $\mathbf{A}_k$ and $\mathbf{B}_k$ collected from each client $k$ on the server side and sending the averaged LoRA modules $\mathbf{\Bar{A}}$ and $\mathbf{\Bar{B}}$ back to clients, LoRA-FAIR reconstructs the ideal global update $\mathbf{\Delta W}$ using \cref{eq:ideal_update}, finds the residual LoRA module $\mathbf{\Delta B}$ using \cref{eq:optimization_problem}, and replaces $\mathbf{\Bar{B}}$ with the corrected LoRA modules $\mathbf{\Bar{B}'} = \mathbf{\Bar{B}} + \mathbf{\Delta B}$. See details in \cref{sec:method}.} \label{fig: lorafair}
elements.sent-2411-009: Emerging foundation models (FMs) \cite{achiam2023gpt, bommasani2021opportunities, touvron2023llama, zeng2022glm, zhou2023comprehensive have demonstrated remarkable capabilities by providing robust and versatile architectures that can be adapted to a wide array of tasks through fine-tuning with task-specific data.}
elements.sent-2411-010: These models excel across diverse applications, including image generation from prompts, language translation, mathematical problem-solving, and natural language conversation, among others \cite{zhou2023comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the standard method of fine-tuning all model parameters, known as full parameter fine-tuning, entails prohibitively high computational costs, particularly for large-scale models.
elements.sent-2411-012: To alleviate this problem, a range of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods \cite{han2024parameter has been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-013: One of the most important PEFT approaches is low-rank adaptation (LoRA) \cite{hu2021lora , which significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters by introducing low-rank matrices into the model.}
elements.sent-2411-014: LoRA introduces a parallel branch of trainable low-rank matrices, \(\mathbf{A}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\), to compute the model update \(\mathbf{\Delta W}\), where the ranks of \(\mathbf{A}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\) are significantly smaller than the parameters of the pre-trained model, \(\mathbf{W}\).
elements.sent-2411-015: In LoRA fine-tuning, only \(\mathbf{A}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\) are updated, while \(\mathbf{W}\) remains frozen.
elements.sent-2411-016: This approach greatly reduces the computational resources required, allowing for efficient fine-tuning with performance comparable to that of full parameter fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite these advantages, LoRA still requires substantial data to adapt effectively to specific downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, data from a single device may not be sufficient for this purpose, and fine-tuning often involves multiple devices that collectively hold the necessary data.
elements.sent-2411-019: This multi-device setup can raise privacy concerns, as fine-tuning with data from multiple parties may expose sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-020: Federated Learning (FL) \cite{mcmahan2017communication offers a feasible solution to this issue.}
elements.sent-2411-021: By enabling collaborative learning without requiring data sharing, FL allows participants to fine-tune models while addressing privacy concerns effectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: Compared to studies on LoRA fine-tuning in centralized settings, fine-tuning LoRA within a federated learning environment remains relatively unexplored and presents unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we investigate traditional FL in conjunction with parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, specifically focusing on LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-024: We argue that fine-tuning LoRA modules presents two key challenges.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, which we refer to as the Challenge 1: Server-Side Aggregation Bias, arises because averaging the LoRA components (\( \mathbf{A \) and \( \mathbf{B \)) independently at the server does not capture the ideal global update, potentially introducing noise into the aggregated model.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Second, the Challenge 2: Client-Side LoRA Initialization Drift highlights the importance of starting each training round with LoRA modules that incorporate the previous round’s averaged global information.
elements.sent-2411-027: Existing FL methods for fine-tuning fail to consider these two key points simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-028: While some methods, such as FLoRA \cite{wang2024flora , attempt to address Challenge 1 by altering the aggregation process, they fail to address Challenge 2, which limits the performance to a level comparable to that of directly combining FedAvg and LoRA (i.e., FedIT \cite{zhang2024towards ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Taking both Challenge 1 and Challenge 2 into consideration simultaneously is essential for maximizing the performance of LoRA fine-tuning in a federated learning setting.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we propose a simple yet effective method, LoRA-FAIR (short for LoRA with Federated Aggregation and Initialization Refinement), designed to tackle both challenges concurrently.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, we propose that, on the server side, the original averaged LoRA modules (e.g., $\mathbf{\bar{A }$ and $\mathbf{\bar{B }$) be kept fixed while introducing a correction term $\mathbf{\Delta B}$ to $\mathbf{\bar{B }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This way, the product of the fine-tuned \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) and \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) will closely approximate the ideal server update.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: To further enhance stability, we introduce a normalization term to ensure that the fine-tuned \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) remains close to its original averaged value, thereby preserving the average information from \( \mathbf{B \) collected from each client.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: This modification not only maintains the global average insights embedded in \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) but also allows \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) to adjust dynamically to approximate the ideal global update \( \mathbf{\Delta W \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Through this simple yet effective design, LoRA-FAIR provides an approach that approximates an ideal solution to both challenges by preserving the shared average information in the initial model while striving for accurate aggregation on the server side.
elements.sent-2411-036: Consequently, LoRA-FAIR maximizes the efficacy of LoRA fine-tuning within an FL framework, balancing performance improvements with computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our key contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-038: We investigate the problem of fine-tuning with LoRA in federated learning setting.
elements.sent-2411-039: Through an initial set of motivation experiments, we identify two key challenges that currently limit the application of LoRA in FL.
elements.sent-2411-040: In response to these challenges, we introduce a novel method named LoRA-FAIR.
elements.sent-2411-041: LoRA-FAIR is the first in the federated fine-tuning domain to simultaneously consider both the two challenges while maintaining computational and communication efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-042: We conduct experiments using two pre-trained foundation models, ViT \cite{dosovitskiy2020image and MLP-Mixer \cite{tolstikhin2021mlp , across various large-scale datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The results demonstrate that our proposed LoRA-FAIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-044: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a PEFT (parameter-efficient fine-tuning) approach that significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters in large-scale models by introducing low-rank matrices into the model.
elements.sent-2411-045: Consider a pre-trained model with parameters $\mathbf{W _0 \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times l}$, where $\mathbf{W _0$ represents the fixed parameters of the model, and $\mathbf{\Delta W \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times l}$ denotes the trainable update matrix applied during fine-tuning.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Rather than updating all elements in $\mathbf{\Delta W}$, LoRA decomposes $\mathbf{\Delta W}$ into two low-rank matrices $\mathbf{A \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times r}$ and $\mathbf{B \in \mathbb{R ^{r \times l}$, where $r \ll \min(d, l)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, the model update is expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\Delta W} = \mathbf{B} \mathbf{A},\end{equation} allowing the fine-tuning process to focus on the much smaller low-rank matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ instead of the full matrix $\mathbf{\Delta W}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Consequently, the total number of parameters that need to be trained is reduced from $d \times l$ to $r \times (d + l)$, where $r$ is significantly smaller than both $d$ and $l$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The updated model parameters after fine-tuning are given by: \begin{equation}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W}_0 + \mathbf{\Delta W} = \mathbf{W}_0 + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{A}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: In practice, $\mathbf{A}$ is typically initialized with random Gaussian values, while $\mathbf{B}$ is initialized to zero to ensure a stable start to the fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-051: This low-rank adaptation enables LoRA to achieve performance comparable to full fine-tuning while significantly reducing the computational and memory overhead.
elements.sent-2411-052: In a standard federated learning setup, multiple clients collaboratively train a shared global model without sharing their local data, thereby preserving privacy.
elements.sent-2411-053: Each client trains on its local data and then transmits its local model updates back to the server, which aggregates these updates to refine the global model.
elements.sent-2411-054: Consider an FL setup with \( K \) clients, starting with an initial model \( \mathbf{W _0 \).}
elements.sent-2411-055: The server collects the local updates from the clients and calculates the global update as follows: \begin{equation}\label{fl_update} \Delta W = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \Delta W_k,\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D _k$ is the client $k$'s local dataset, the weights \( p_k = \frac{|\mathcal{D _k| {\sum_{k |\mathcal{D _k| \) are proportional to the size of each client’s local dataset, and \( \mathbf{\Delta W _k \) denotes the local update from client \( k \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: To start the next round of local training, the server uses the global update \( \mathbf{\Delta W \) to generate an updated global model, which is then distributed to each client as the initial model for the subsequent round.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The next round of training for each client can be represented as follows, assuming clients train for \( E \) epochs during local training: \begin{equation}\label{fl_initial} W_{k,0} = W_0 + \Delta W; \nonumber W_{k,e+1} = W_{k,e} - \eta g_{k,e}, \quad e = 0, \dots, E-1; \nonumber \Delta W_k = -\sum_{e=0}^{E-1} \eta g_{k,e},\end{equation} where \( \eta \) is the local learning rate, and \( g_{k,e \) represents the stochastic gradient for client \( k \) at epoch \( e \).}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{fl_initial} W_{k,0} = W_0 + \Delta W; \nonumber W_{k,e+1} = W_{k,e} - \eta g_{k,e}, \quad e = 0, \dots, E-1; \nonumber \Delta W_k = -\sum_{e=0}^{E-1} \eta g_{k,e},\end{equation}
elements.section-2411-003: Challenges when Combining LoRA with Federated Learning
elements.sent-2411-058: Fine-tuning foundation models in federated learning using full-parameter updates aligns with traditional FL methods.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, incorporating LoRA introduces unique challenges that diverge from those in centralized settings.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/challenge_1.pdf} \caption{Comparison of two aggregation strategies: AvgToMul and MulToAvg. AvgToMul averages the LoRA matrices $\mathbf{A}_k$ and $\mathbf{B}_k$ from clients, then multiplies the averages to obtain the approximate global update $\mathbf{\Delta W}'$ using \cref{eq:approx_update}. MulToAvg first multiplies each client’s matrices (yielding $\mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{A}_k$) and then averages these products for the true global update $\mathbf{\Delta W}$ using \cref{eq:ideal_update}. While AvgToMul is communication-efficient, MulToAvg better captures the intended global model update. See details in \cref{sec:challenge1}.} \label{fig:c1}
elements.sent-2411-060: To discuss this challenge, we first introduce a basic method that combines LoRA directly with FL, known as FedIT \cite{zhang2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-061: In FedIT, each of the $K$ clients starts with a fixed pre-trained foundation model $\mathbf{W _0$ and trains the local LoRA modules represented as low-rank matrices \( \mathbf{A _k \) and \( \mathbf{B _k \) on its private dataset $\mathcal{D _k$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The server then aggregates these local matrices uploaded by clients into global LoRA modules, $\mathbf{\bar{A }$ and $\mathbf{\bar{B }$, through a weighted average based on data size: \begin{equation}\label{global_modules} \mathbf{\bar{A}} = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{A}_k, \quad \mathbf{\bar{B}} = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{B}_k,\end{equation} where \( p_k = \frac{|\mathcal{D _k| {\sum_{k=1 ^K |\mathcal{D _k| \) reflects each client's data proportion.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Using these averaged matrices, the server distributes them back to the clients for subsequent training rounds.
elements.sent-2411-064: In FedIT, the actual global update received by each client is: \begin{equation}\label{eq:approx_update} \mathbf{\Delta W}' = \mathbf{\bar{B}} \mathbf{\bar{A}} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{B}_k \right) \left( \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{A}_k \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, this aggregated update deviates from the ideal global model update in the typical FL setting, which should be the weighted sum of all local model updates: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ideal_update} \mathbf{\Delta W} = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{\Delta W}_k = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{A}_k \neq \mathbf{\Delta W}'.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ideal_update} \Delta W = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \Delta W_k = \sum_{k=1}^K p_k B_k A_k \neq \Delta W'.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: This discrepancy, termed Server-Side Aggregation Bias, occurs because the approximate global update $\mathbf{\Delta W '$ fails to accurately capture the ideal global update $\mathbf{\Delta W}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: To demonstrate this, we compare the two aggregation methods under a single global round with 50 local epochs independent of the client-side initialization on the DomainNet dataset.
elements.sent-2411-068: As shown in \cref{fig:c1 , AvgToMul and MulToAvg denotes the aggregated update using $\mathbf{\Delta W '$ and $\mathbf{\Delta W}$ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Although AvgToMul reduces communication costs by only transmitting the LoRA modules, it does so at the expense of alignment with the intended global model update.
elements.sent-2411-070: This challenge highlights the need for more refined aggregation methods when integrating LoRA into FL frameworks.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/challenge_2.pdf} \caption{Comparison of three initialization strategies: Avg-Initial, Re-Initial, Local-Initial. The Avg-Initial method is the most effective as it balances continuity and unification across clients, reducing initialization drift and promoting better convergence. For more details, refer to \cref{sec:challenge2}.} \label{fig:c2}
elements.sent-2411-071: To mitigate server-side aggregation bias, FFA-LoRA \cite{sun2024improving was proposed, freezing the non-zero initialized low-rank matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and updating only the zero-initialized matrix $\mathbf{B}$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, this approach slows fine-tuning and limits performance due to fewer trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-073: A more recent method, FLoRA \cite{wang2024flora , stacks local LoRA modules from all clients and transmits the stacked LoRA modules back to each client to reconstruct global updates, which are then added directly to each local pre-trained model while reinitializing local LoRA modules for the next training round.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Although FLoRA addresses Challenge 1 effectively, it incurs high communication cost proportional to the number of clients and poses privacy concerns, as it requires distributing all clients' LoRA modules to each client rather than only the averaged modules, as in FedIT.
elements.sent-2411-075: Furthermore, FLoRA's reinitialization strategy for LoRA modules (typically randomizing $\mathbf{A}$ with Gaussian distribution and setting $\mathbf{B}$ to zero) introduces Client-Side Initialization Drift.
elements.sent-2411-076: The gradient of \(\mathbf{A}\) depends on the current state of \(\mathbf{B}\), and the gradient of \(\mathbf{B}\) similarly depends on \(\mathbf{A}\).
elements.sent-2411-077: This interdependence implies that updates to one matrix are influenced by the configuration of the other, affecting the fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-078: Frequent reinitialization produces small gradient updates, leading to inefficient training and potentially suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-079: To understand the effects of different client-side initialization methods on model performance, we evaluate three strategies in an FL setup with 6 clients, each assigned a unique domain from the DomainNet dataset.
elements.sent-2411-080: To isolate the impact of client-side initialization, we ensure that in all methods the server performs the same aggregation method, \(\Delta W'\), and each client adjusts the pre-trained model part accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-081: The three strategies are as follows: 1.
elements.sent-2411-082: Avg-Initial: The averaged LoRA modules aggregated from all clients are used as the initialization for the next round, maintaining parameter continuity across rounds and achieving the best performance.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-083: Re-Initial: LoRA modules are reinitialized at each round (with random Gaussian values for \(\mathbf{A}\) and zeros for \(\mathbf{B}\)).
elements.sent-2411-084: This approach helps prevent overfitting to client-specific data but limits gradient updates, slowing convergence.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-085: Local-Initial: A randomly selected client's last-round local LoRA modules are used as the starting LoRA point for all clients.
elements.sent-2411-086: As shown in \cref{fig:c2 , the Avg-Initial method is the most effective, as it balances continuity and unification across clients, reducing initialization drift and promoting stable convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-087: By averaging local LoRA modules, this method captures a representative update, smoothing extreme deviations and fostering a consistent training path.
elements.sent-2411-088: In summary, both server-side aggregation bias and client-side initialization drift are substantial challenges when combining LoRA with federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-089: Addressing these issues is critical for effective foundation model fine-tuning in FL settings, ensuring performance and convergence stability.
elements.section-2411-004: LoRA-FAIR: A Simple but Effective Solution
elements.sent-2411-090: Building on the challenges outlined in previous sections, we propose a novel aggregation mechanism, LoRA-FAIR (shown in \cref{fig: lorafair ), designed to address both server-side aggregation bias and client-side initialization drift simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-091: LoRA-FAIR employs a residual-based approach to refine the global model update.
elements.sent-2411-092: Rather than relying solely on the averaged LoRA matrices \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) and \( \mathbf{\bar{B \), LoRA-FAIR introduces a correction term for \( \mathbf{\bar{B \), denoted as the residual LoRA module \( \mathbf{\Delta B \), to tackle both the server-side and client-side issues concurrently.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Notably, LoRA-FAIR refines the global LoRA matrices at the server, without introducing additional communication or computational costs on the client side.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section, we outline the key steps of LoRA-FAIR and demonstrate how it simultaneously addresses both Challenge 1 and Challenge 2.
elements.sent-2411-095: To illustrate the process, consider a FL setup with \( K \) clients participating in fine-tuning at round \( t+1 \).. Server Side.
elements.sent-2411-096: After fine-tuning in round \( t \), each client \( k \) sends its locally fine-tuned LoRA modules \( \mathbf{A _k \) and \( \mathbf{B _k \) back to the server.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The server first aggregates these local modules to obtain the global modules \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) and \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) using \cref{global_modules .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Rather than directly distributing \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) and \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) to the clients, LoRA-FAIR refines the server-side aggregation by introducing a residual update \( \mathbf{\Delta B \), optimizing the following: \begin{equation}\label{eq:optimization_problem} \arg\min\limits_{\mathbf{\Delta B}} \underbrace{\mathcal{S}\left(\mathbf{\Delta W}, (\mathbf{\bar{B}} + \mathbf{\Delta B}) \mathbf{\bar{A}}\right)}_{\text{correction}} + \underbrace{\lambda ||\mathbf{\Delta B}||}_{\text{regularization}},\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{\Delta W \) represents the ideal global update from \cref{eq:ideal_update , and \( \mathcal{S (\cdot) \) is a similarity metric (cosine similarity \cite{chowdhury2010introduction in our experiments) that measures the discrepancy between \( (\mathbf{\bar{B + \mathbf{\Delta B ) \mathbf{\bar{A \) and \( \mathbf{\Delta W \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: We denote the corrected averaged LoRA \(\mathbf{B}\) with the residual as \(\mathbf{\bar{B ' = \mathbf{\bar{B + \mathbf{\Delta B}\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The regularization weight \( \lambda \) balances the correction term and the regularization term.
elements.sent-2411-101: Rather than directly finding an analytical solution, we can use methods such as SGD to obtain a solution, which is computationally feasible and efficient.
elements.sent-2411-102: Upon determining \( \mathbf{\Delta B \), the server distributes \(\mathbf{\bar{B ' = \mathbf{\bar{B + \mathbf{\Delta B}\) and \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) to the clients for the next training round.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This approach introduces no additional communication costs.
elements.sent-2411-104: Unlike existing methods that require large-matrix SVD computations \cite{bai2024federated or transmission of all client-stacked LoRA modules \cite{wang2024flora , LoRA-FAIR achieves computational and communication efficiency.. Client Side.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Once client \( k \) receives $\mathbf{\bar{B '}$ and \( \mathbf{\bar{A \), it begins local fine-tuning for round \( t+1 \) using its local dataset.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The client uses $\mathbf{\bar{B '}$ as the initialization for its LoRA module \( \mathbf{B _k \) and \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) as the initialization for \( \mathbf{A _k \).}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-107: LoRA-FAIR tackles the server-side aggregation bias by introducing the residual correction term \( \mathbf{\Delta B \), which refines the aggregated LoRA matrix \( \mathbf{\bar{B \) on the server.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast to straightforward averaging, which leads to \( \mathbf{\bar{B \mathbf{\bar{A \) diverging from the ideal global update \( \mathbf{\Delta W = \sum_{k=1 ^K p_k \mathbf{B _k \mathbf{A _k \), LoRA-FAIR computes a residual update that minimizes the difference between the aggregated update and the ideal.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: By optimizing \( \mathbf{\Delta B \), LoRA-FAIR approximates the target global model update more accurately, reducing the bias introduced by direct averaging.}
elements.sent-2411-110: This correction ensures that the server-generated update better captures the interactions between local LoRA matrices, aligning \( (\mathbf{\bar{B + \mathbf{\Delta B ) \mathbf{\bar{A \) with the true aggregated update.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: LoRA-FAIR also addresses the client-side initialization drift by distributing the refined LoRA matrices $\mathbf{\bar{B '}$ and \( \mathbf{\bar{A \) to each client, ensuring that each round begins with globally-informed parameters.}}}

elements.sent-2411-180: The increasing size of foundation models makes full-parameter fine-tuning computationally and storage-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-181: To address these challenges, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods \cite{fu2023effectiveness, ding2023parameter, liu2022few, han2024parameter have been proposed to reduce the number of trainable parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-182: PEFT techniques introduce a limited set of additional trainable parameters to enhance model performance while keeping most pre-trained parameters frozen.
elements.sent-2411-183: Some approaches, such as \cite{houlsby2019parameter , add trainable parameters called adapters to each layer of the pre-trained network, updating only the adapters during fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Other approaches, like \cite{cai2020tinytl , focus on fine-tuning only the bias terms of the pre-trained model.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Techniques such as prefix-tuning \cite{li2021prefix and prompt-tuning \cite{lester2021power add trainable dimensions to the input or hidden layers of the network.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Among PEFT methods, a key approach is LoRA \cite{hu2021lora , which uses low-rank matrices to approximate the pre-trained weight matrix, updating only the low-rank matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-187: In this paper, we utilize LoRA as our PEFT method due to its demonstrated efficiency, achieving comparable performance to full-parameter fine-tuning while modifying fewer than 5\% of the parameters.. Federated Learning.
elements.sent-2411-188: FedAvg \cite{mcmahan2017communication , the foundational work in FL, demonstrates the advantages of this approach in terms of privacy and communication efficiency by aggregating local model parameters to train a shared global model.}

elements.sent-2411-196: However, these methods do not address client initialization drift.
elements.sent-2411-197: In this work, we proposed LoRA-FAIR to address the key challenges of server-side aggregation bias and client-side initialization drift in federated fine-tuning with LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-198: LoRA-FAIR approximates an ideal solution by maintaining shared average information while ensuring dynamic server-side adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our experiments on large-scale datasets demonstrated its superior performance over state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-200: Future work will explore extending LoRA-FAIR beyond computer vision datasets and adapting it for scenarios where clients use different LoRA ranks to enhance its applicability in diverse federated learning environments.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-201: However, combining LoRA with FL introduces two key challenges: the Server-Side Aggregation Bias, where server-side averaging of LoRA matrices diverges from the ideal global update, and the Client-Side Initialization Lag, emphasizing the need for consistent initialization across rounds.
elements.sent-2411-202: We propose LoRA-FAIR, a novel method that tackles both issues by introducing a correction term on the server, enhancing aggregation efficiency and accuracy.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/illustration.pdf} \caption{Illustration of LoRA-FAIR. Instead of directly averaging the local LoRA modules $\mathbf{A}_k$ and $\mathbf{B}_k$ collected from each client $k$ on the server side and sending the averaged LoRA modules $\mathbf{\Bar{A}}$ and $\mathbf{\Bar{B}}$ back to clients, LoRA-FAIR reconstructs the ideal global update $\mathbf{\Delta W}$ using \cref{eq:ideal_update}, finds the residual LoRA module $\mathbf{\Delta B}$ using \cref{eq:optimization_problem}, and replaces $\mathbf{\Bar{B}}$ with the corrected LoRA modules $\mathbf{\Bar{B}'} = \mathbf{\Bar{B}} + \mathbf{\Delta B}$. See details in \cref{sec:method}.} \label{fig: lorafair}

elements.subsection-2411-011: Adaptation for Clients with Heterogeneous LoRA Ranks
elements.sent-2411-266: Our proposed method primarily focuses on settings where clients have the same LoRA rank, addressing challenges such as server aggregation bias and client initialization lag when combining LoRA with federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-267: However, our approach can be extended to scenarios where clients have heterogeneous LoRA ranks.
elements.sent-2411-268: The state-of-the-art method for handling heterogeneous ranks in FL is HETLoRA \cite{cho2023heterogeneous , which employs zero-padding and truncation for distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-269: It is important to note that HETLoRA is specifically designed for heterogeneous settings and operates orthogonally to our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-270: By integrating zero-padding and truncation for distribution into LoRA-FAIR, our method can effectively operate in heterogeneous rank settings.
elements.sent-2411-271: We evaluate this adaptation using the DomainNet dataset with ViT as the foundation model.
elements.sent-2411-272: The client data distribution and training settings are consistent with those used in the feature non-IID experiments in the main paper.
elements.sent-2411-273: The clients LoRA ranks are set as $\{2, 4, 4, 6, 6, 8\}$.
elements.sent-2411-274: The results, presented in \cref{tab: heter_rank , demonstrate that our proposed method, combined with zero-padding and truncation, achieves the best performance compared to existing methods, validating its effectiveness in heterogeneous rank scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-275: We note that due to the heterogeneous LoRA ranks, FedIT and FFA-LoRA are not suitable for this setting and are therefore excluded from the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-276: While FLoRA can operate under heterogeneous settings, it is not included in the results as it fails to converge in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-277: This failure underscores its limitation of directly adding updates to the pre-trained model rather than updating the LoRA modules.

elements.sent-2411-278: Our proposed method addresses both server aggregation bias and client initialization lag by solving \cref{eq:optimization_problem , introducing only a small computational overhead on the server side.}
elements.sent-2411-279: In our main experiment, we solve \cref{eq:optimization_problem using SGD with a learning rate of 0.01 and 1000 iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-280: However, this additional cost is minimal and can be considered negligible given the substantial computational resources typically available on servers.
elements.sent-2411-281: Moreover, a comparison of training times, as shown in \cref{fig:computation , demonstrates that the time required to solve \cref{eq:optimization_problem on the server is minimal compared to the client-side local training time.}}
elements.sent-2411-282: We evaluate performance under different local epochs.
elements.sent-2411-283: In our main experiments with the feature non-IID setting, we set the number of local epochs to 2 and the number of global rounds to 50.
elements.sent-2411-284: Here, we test a configuration where the local epochs are set to 10, and accordingly, the global rounds are reduced to 10 to maintain a fixed total number of updates.
elements.sent-2411-285: The results indicate that a shorter local epoch with more frequent updates leads to better performance for both the proposed method and FLoRA.
elements.sent-2411-286: This finding is consistent with \cite{zhao2018federated , which suggests that the number of local epochs should not be too high in a non-IID FL setting.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Furthermore, with shorter local epochs, the performance gap between our proposed method and FLoRA increases, further validating that FLoRA's reinitialization strategy fails to learn an optimal client update under limited local training, ultimately degrading the final model performance.
elements.sent-2411-288: In this section, we review existing methods and their limitations.
elements.sent-2411-289: FedIT \cite{zhang2024towards : FedIT is the earliest approach to integrate LoRA with FedAvg.}
elements.sent-2411-290: In FedIT, each client starts with a fixed pre-trained foundation model and trains local LoRA modules, represented as low-rank matrices \( \mathbf{A _k \) and \( \mathbf{B _k \), on its private dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-291: The server aggregates these local matrices into global LoRA modules through a weighted average based on data size.
elements.sent-2411-292: While computationally efficient, this method introduces server-side aggregation bias.
elements.sent-2411-293: FFA-LoRA \cite{sun2024improving : FFA-LoRA freezes the non-zero initialized low-rank matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) and updates only the zero-initialized matrix \(\mathbf{B}\).}
elements.sent-2411-294: By freezing \(\mathbf{A}\), the actual global update becomes equal to the ideal global update (i.e., \(\mathbf{\Delta W = \mathbf{\Delta W '\)), addressing server-side aggregation bias.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: However, freezing \(\mathbf{A}\) significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters, limiting the model's capacity.
elements.sent-2411-296: Our experiments confirm that although FFA-LoRA resolves aggregation bias, its limited parameter flexibility results in worse performance compared to other baselines.
elements.sent-2411-297: FLoRA \cite{wang2024flora : FLoRA stacks local LoRA modules from all clients and transmits the stacked modules back to each client to reconstruct global updates, which are then added directly to each client's pre-trained model while reinitializing local LoRA modules for the next training round.}
elements.sent-2411-298: Although FLoRA effectively addresses server-side aggregation bias, it incurs high communication costs proportional to the number of clients and raises privacy concerns, as it distributes all clients' LoRA modules rather than only the averaged ones.
elements.sent-2411-299: Additionally, FLoRA's reinitialization strategy introduces Client-Side Initialization Lag.
elements.sent-2411-300: Frequent reinitialization results in small gradient updates, leading to inefficient training and suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-301: FlexLoRA \cite{bai2024federated : FlexLoRA reformulates each client’s local LoRA modules into a local update, sums these updates to generate a global update, and applies SVD to produce global LoRA modules.}
elements.sent-2411-302: These modules are then distributed to clients as initialization for the next round.
elements.sent-2411-303: While this approach formulates an ideal global update, it still suffers from server-side aggregation bias due to the SVD step.
elements.sent-2411-304: For example, consider two clients, each with rank-8 LoRA modules (\( \text{Rank (\Delta W_1) = 8 \) and \( \text{Rank (\Delta W_2) = 8 \)), resulting in a global update with \(\text{Rank (\Delta W) \leq 16\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-305: Using SVD to produce global modules with a rank of 8 may lead to information loss, preventing the transmission of an ideal global update to clients.
elements.sent-2411-306: Comparison to Existing Efficient Weight Aggregation FL Methods.
elements.sent-2411-307: Our work identifies a gap in existing federated learning methods concerning fine-tuning with LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-308: While prior approaches—such as layer-wise model aggregation~\cite{ma2022layer , elastic aggregation~\cite{chen2023elastic , and related layer-wise techniques~\cite{rehman2023dawa, lee2023layer —have demonstrated effectiveness in general federated optimization, they are not well-suited for LoRA-based fine-tuning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-309: Specifically, these methods do not address how to decompose aggregated model updates into the necessary LoRA modules for client model initialization, nor do they provide strategies to avoid the direct transmission of these updates.
elements.sent-2411-310: To overcome these limitations, our method tailors the aggregation process specifically for federated fine-tuning with LoRA, bridging the gap left by existing techniques.
